beats.activity: Wakes up, makes tea, journals by the window in soft morning light
beats.reasoning: Maya is an early riser who loves this ritual—tea, journaling, watching the city wake. Starts the day grounded and mindful, avoiding screens. This is her sacred morning anchor.

beats.activity: Sunrise walk and meditation at Gas Works Park
beats.reasoning: Perfect warm Friday morning—ideal for her beloved sunrise ritual. A short walk from Fremont, this grounds her before teaching. She's done this three days in a row; it's her anchor practice.

beats.activity: Commute to CorePower Yoga for morning class prep
beats.reasoning: Travel from Wallingford to Capitol Hill; she needs time to set up the studio, center herself, and prepare for the first class. About 20–25 min drive in light morning traffic.

beats.activity: Teach morning vinyasa class at CorePower; Teach power yoga class
beats.reasoning: Maya typically teaches multiple back-to-back classes. A warm Friday morning is perfect for a flow class; students will be energized by the nice weather.

beats.activity: Break between classes; hydrate and reset
beats.reasoning: Maya needs breathing room between classes. She'll hydrate (77F is warm), maybe stretch gently, center herself. No screens—she respects her no-screens-before-noon rule.

beats.activity: Teach noon power or hot class
beats.reasoning: Third class of her teaching day. The noon slot is typically her busiest; warm weather brings people in. This is her rhythm from recent days.

beats.activity: Grab smoothie bowl and sit mindfully, phone off
beats.reasoning: Maya's go-to ritual after teaching. She sits with her bowl, journaling or meditating, deliberately offline. This is her restorative anchor—she does this most days and loves it.
Capitol Hill to Fremont is a 20–25 min drive on a Friday afternoon. She's heading home to rest before evening; warm weather and Pride crowds will build downtown, making early escape smart.

beats.activity: Afternoon rest: stretch on mat, light reading, journaling
beats.reasoning: After three intense classes, Maya needs restoration. She stretches, reads, maybe naps. This mirrors her pattern from recent days—self-care in the afternoon golden light is non-negotiable for her energy.

beats.activity: Nature walk through Woodland Park, noticing small details
beats.reasoning: Warm, breezy Friday is perfect for a mindful walk. Maya loves this park and has walked here recently. Close to Fremont, no screens, aligns with her nature-loving personality and recent journaling practice.

beats.activity: Commute to Capitol Hill for Pride street fair, explore and enjoy the energy; Enjoy Pride street fair: walk, eat, socialize, soak in the festive energy
beats.reasoning: Seattle Pride kickoff on a warm Friday evening—Broadway and Pike are alive with festivities. Maya is 24, mindful, community-oriented; she'd naturally want to soak in the vibe, connect with people, and celebrate. Leaves Fremont by 4:45pm to arrive before peak crowds and road closures intensify at 5pm.

beats.activity: Commute home and wind down; read in bed, then sleep
beats.reasoning: Maya heads home after Pride (about 25 min from Capitol Hill), unwinds with light reading or journaling to process the day, and sleeps. She's an early riser, so 6.5 hours aligns with her rhythm. The overnight sleep beat ends just before her typical wake time.

diary:
Woke early to soft light filtering through my Fremont apartment, tea steaming while I journaled. Gas Works was perfect this morning—that quiet meditation before the city wakes up feels like a reset button. Teaching back-to-back classes at CorePower left me buzzing but good; there's something about guiding people through their bodies that never gets old.

Afternoon was mine. I stretched at home, wandered through Woodland Park and noticed the way light hit the ferns. Small things. Then Capitol Hill called—Pride street fair was electric. The energy was infectious, rainbow flags everywhere, music spilling from corners. I walked with friends, ate something greasy and delicious, and just let myself be part of something bigger. It felt like the whole neighborhood was breathing together. Heading home now, still riding that warmth.
thought_process: This morning I needed grounding before the teaching rush, so I prioritized the early walk—it's become non-negotiable for me. Teaching three classes is intense but purposeful; I was present for each one. Around midday I felt the need to slow down, to be alone with myself, which is why the Woodland Park walk mattered more than rushing back. But when evening came and I thought about Pride happening live on my doorstep, I realized I didn't want to miss it. The festive energy of the city pulled me back out. It wasn't planned; it was intuitive. I needed both the quiet and the celebration today—the contrast made sense.
world_event_prompt: Warm, breezy Friday, high of 77F. Seattle Pride kickoff: Capitol Hill street fair and road closures along Broadway and Pike from 5pm. Festive, busy evening downtown.
